title: Water for the High Herds
subtitle: Water Management for Livestock in the High Hills and Mountains
location_line: Annapurna Rural Municipality  |  Myagdi District  |  Gandaki Province
outro: Identification and Preliminary Study of Water Management
for Livestock in High Hills and Mountains
Ministry of Energy, Water Resources and Water Supply, Gandaki Province  |  2083 BS

sentences: In the high pastures of Myagdi District, in the shadow of the Annapurna massif, thousands of sheep, goats, and yak migrate each season along routes their herders have followed for generations.
Here, above two thousand five hundred metres, the seasons are governed by snow, grass, and water.
For the herders of Annapurna Rural Municipality, Ward Five, access to clean water is not a convenience. It is survival.
This is a preliminary study of water management for livestock in the high hills and mountains, commissioned by the Ministry of Energy, Water Resources and Water Supply of Gandaki Province.
The study area lies in Ward Five of Annapurna Rural Municipality, around the settlements of Kindu, Pauddar, and Nabi.
It focuses on three pasture stations, or kharkas, known as Naribang, Hile Danda, and Ratamata.
These kharkas sit between two thousand seven hundred and three thousand nine hundred metres in elevation.
Two pasture circuits shape the herding year.
The Pauddar circuit ascends through Ekpate, Dhakutri, and Hile Danda.
The Kindu circuit moves through Demorlote, Ratamata, and Pholira.
Together, they support more than thirteen hundred small ruminants, and two hundred and twenty-two yak.
This is the majority of Myagdi District's entire chauri and yak population.
The study combined field survey, community consultation, flow gauging, water-quality testing, and satellite-based mapping of the sources, routes, and settlements.
Life in these mountains is shaped by the movement of water, and that movement is changing.
Each year, the snowline retreats earlier, and rainfall patterns shift.
The springs and streams that herders once depended on diminish, or dry entirely, during the lean season.
Along some stretches of the migration route, animals must walk four to six hours without water.
This strain reduces milk production, lowers birth rates, and increases the risk of disease.
The traditional watering points, locally known as Donths, are simple stone-lined basins, open and unprotected.
Livestock can step directly into the water, fouling and contaminating the only source available.
Yet the need is constant. Cold-altitude animals require between twenty and forty-five litres of clean water per head, every single day, to hold their condition.
The people of these kharkas have practised transhumance, the seasonal movement of herds, for generations.
This study identified thirteen active livestock operators, families whose primary livelihood is sheep, goat, and yak.
Among them is a remarkable institution: the Chauri Srot Conservation and Promotion Committee.
This cooperative operates one hundred and fifty yak at Khopra Danda, at three thousand six hundred and sixty metres.
It was founded through an initiative of Ramon Magsaysay Award laureate Mahabir Pun.
Its purpose is unusual. The cooperative funds teacher salaries at two local schools.
Income comes from three sources: gourmet cheese sold to trekking lodges, a community festival, and eco-lodges for visitors.
Roughly forty percent of the schools' operating costs are met from this single livestock enterprise.
It is a powerful demonstration that, with the right water and the right organisation, high-mountain herding can sustain far more than the herders alone.
At the heart of this study is a simple question. Where can clean, reliable water be found, and is there enough of it?
Two principal sources were identified, each able to supply the kharkas year-round.
Source A is the perennial spring of Dhan Kharka Mathi Kuna Mul, at three thousand two hundred and forty-five metres, on the Pauddar and Kindu circuit.
Its design safe yield is enough to supply all six kharkas on that circuit, with more than twice the demand available as surplus.
Source B is Jhangaji Khola, at four thousand and ten metres, which supplies Naribang.
Water from both springs was collected and tested by an accredited national laboratory.
The results are exceptional.
Both springs are extremely soft and low in minerals, with total dissolved solids of just twenty-four to thirty-seven milligrams per litre.
The pH is near neutral, turbidity is negligible, and the E. coli count is zero.
Every measured parameter falls within Nepal's National Drinking Water Quality Standards. This is pristine, high-altitude spring water, fully suitable for livestock.
How much water is needed, and how can it be delivered?
Design demand was calculated for each scheme, fifteen years into the future.
For Scheme A, serving Hile Danda and Ratamata, the design demand is fifty-four thousand three hundred and thirty litres per day.
For Scheme B, serving Naribang, it is twelve thousand nine hundred and ninety litres per day.
These figures are based on forty-five litres per day for each yak, and twenty litres per day for each sheep or goat.
The proposed solution is elegant in its simplicity: two gravity-fed pipeline schemes, with no pumps, no electricity, and no recurring energy cost.
Scheme A carries spring water through a three thousand seven hundred metre pipeline, descending the mountain through four break-pressure tanks.
It feeds twelve improved Donths and six tap stand posts.
Scheme B uses a shorter pipeline to feed three Donths, at the yak pasture, the sheep pasture, and the livestock shelter.
At the heart of the design is the improved Donth itself.
It formalises the traditional trough into a dressed-stone or concrete basin, with inlet control, a silt trap, and a sanitary cover.
Animals can drink freely, but can no longer enter or contaminate the water.
The combined cost of both schemes is five point seven seven million Nepali Rupees.
The Provincial Government would contribute ninety percent; the water user associations, the remaining ten.
Infrastructure alone does not guarantee success. This study examined sustainability across four dimensions.
Technically, gravity flow requires only routine maintenance, and the high-density pipe has a service life beyond fifty years.
Financially, the cooperative model already operating in these mountains shows that livestock enterprise can comfortably sustain operation and maintenance costs.
Organizationally, the study recommends that women hold at least forty percent of the seats on every water user committee.
Environmentally, the health of the rangeland, and the protection of the spring recharge zones, are identified as complementary priorities.
The high pastures of Myagdi are not marginal land.
They are the foundation of a pastoral economy that has sustained communities for centuries, and that today supplies cheese, meat, and wool to markets across the region.
With reliable water, a gravity pipeline, a clean source, a protected trough, the herders of Ward Five can work with certainty, rather than hope.
This study recommends the immediate implementation of both schemes, as a priority programme for the Provincial Government.
The water is there. The demand is clear. The community is ready.
